meaning: To make an image appear larger and closer on a screen
example: You can zoom in to see the details of the photograph.
example2: The camera has a powerful 10x optical zoom.
example3: I had to zoom out to see the whole map.
Explanation: Zoom is a common function in digital photography, video, and software interfaces. It changes the 'magnification' of what you are looking at. There are two types: 'optical zoom,' which uses a physical lens to get closer, and 'digital zoom,' which simply stretches the pixels of the image. In software, 'zooming' is used to focus on small parts of a document or image. It is also the name of a very popular video conferencing app, though as a verb, it just refers to the action of changing the view size.
usageTip: Use 'zoom in' to see more detail and 'zoom out' to see the bigger picture.
commonMistake: Confusing 'digital zoom' with quality; digital zoom often makes images look blurry.

meaning: To magnify or scale a specific area of a screen graphical user interface to view details more closely, or to shrink it to see a wider view
example: The user can pinch the screen to zoom into the high-resolution product map.
example2: If the text size is too small, you can zoom the browser window to 120 percent.
example3: The maps application lets you zoom out to view the entire city layout at once.
Explanation: The term 'zoom' refers to adjusting the magnification scale of digital interfaces, images, text, or web layouts. It encompasses two primary actions: 'zoom in', which enlarges an area to show fine details, and 'zoom out', which shrinks the viewport scale to offer a broader look. Ensuring that your responsive web layouts remain functional and perfectly readable when users zoom their browsers up to 200 percent is a vital requirement for digital accessibility guidelines (WCAG).
commonContext: Browser settings, accessibility design, map applications, photo viewing
usageTip: Always pair 'zoom' with prepositions like 'in' or 'out' to accurately define the direction of magnification scaling.
commonMistake: Capitalizing 'zoom' in general prose when you mean magnification, confusing it with the video conferencing application called Zoom.
